https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Special to Darkhorse Press
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
STARKVILLE — British Consul General to the Southeast U.S. Rachel Galloway visited Mississippi State today for a discussion with university President Mark E. Keenum on leadership and shared U.K.-American cultural and education connections, including the highly coveted Marshall Scholarship.

Their meeting followed a day of tours and talks across campus for the Consulate who viewed MSU’s Ulysses S. Grant Presidential Library, Raspet Flight Research Laboratory and the Advanced Composites Institute, ending the day in talks with several of the state’s aerospace and defense company leaders.

Galloway also took part in a Shackouls Honors College Lunch and Learn where she reminded students and faculty of her country’s Marshall Scholarship opportunity. The prestigious program pays for up to 50 select American graduate students to study at a U.K. university each year. MSU 2024 biological sciences graduate Madison Brode of Memphis, Tennessee, was the university’s first Marshall recipient in 2023. The honors college annually provides pathways for students to excel in global study, leadership and service, and the university’s Office of Prestigious External Scholarships supports MSU students applying for nationally and internationally competitive scholarships and fellowships.

A former ambassador to the Republic of North Macedonia, Galloway began serving as Consul General in Atlanta, Georgia, in 2022. She is one of seven U.K. consuls general in the U.S., not including the country’s embassy in Washington, D.C.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: STARKVILLE — British Consul General to the Southeast U.S. Rachel Galloway visited Mississippi State today for a discussion with university President Mark E. Keenum on leadership and shared U.K.-American cultural and education connections, including the highly coveted Marshall Scholarship.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Mississippi State welcomes British Consul General to campus - Darkhorse Press

A woman from Mississippi was killed Tuesday night in a crash involving a train in Shelby County, according to the Shelby County Sheriff’s Office.

WBRC reported a large law enforcement presence near the railroad crossing on County Road 433 in Chelsea shortly before 9 p.m. Tuesday.

The Shelby County Coroner’s Office identified the woman as 31-year-old Michelle A. Manning, who had been living in Birmingham recently, according to WBRC.

The Shelby County Sheriff’s Office said the crossing would be closed “for an extended period of time,” and later reported it reopened by about 10:30 p.m., according to WBRC.

Deputies urged drivers to avoid the area and take alternate routes while the scene was secured.

WBRC reported the Alabama Law Enforcement Agency is investigating the crash.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: A woman from Mississippi was killed Tuesday night in a crash involving a train in Shelby County, according to the Shelby County Sheriff’s Office.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Mississippi woman killed in train crash at Shelby County crossing - Darkhorse Press

TUPELO – A motorcyclist was killed in a traffic crash Tuesday on North Gloster Street, according to the Tupelo Police Department.

Police said officers responded to a fatal crash involving a vehicle and a motorcycle near the Mississippi Department of Transportation offices on North Gloster Street.

Authorities initially shut down North Gloster Street due to the heavy police presence and investigation at the scene. In an update, police said the roadway has since reopened and the scene has been cleared.

No additional details about the victim or the circumstances of the crash were immediately released. Police said more information will be provided as it becomes available.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: TUPELO – A motorcyclist was killed in a traffic crash Tuesday on North Gloster Street, according to the Tupelo Police Department.

NATCHEZ — The Adams County Sheriff’s Office said a Natchez woman has been arrested in connection with a credit card fraud investigation.

According to the sheriff’s office, a deputy conducted a traffic stop at approximately 9:24 a.m. Feb. 25 on West Stiers Lane. During the stop, authorities said it was determined the driver, 29-year-old Mahalia Bruce of Natchez, had an outstanding warrant issued by the Adams County Justice Court.

The warrant stems from an incident reported Feb. 24 in which Bruce is accused of unlawfully using a credit card number without the cardholder’s consent to make purchases at Walmart and Sonic in Adams County, authorities said. The charge listed is fraud involving a credit card.

Bruce was taken into custody without incident and transported to the Adams County Jail, the sheriff’s office said. She was booked on charges of credit card fraud and embezzlement involving agents, trustees, clerks or other persons in a position of trust. She is being held pending an initial appearance before a judge.

The sheriff’s office noted that all individuals are presumed innocent unless proven guilty in a court of law.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: NATCHEZ — The Adams County Sheriff’s Office said a Natchez woman has been arrested in connection with a credit card fraud investigation.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Natchez woman arrested in Adams County credit card fraud investigation - Darkhorse Press

PASCAGOULA — Law enforcement is investigating a threat discovered Thursday morning at Ingalls Shipbuilding on the Mississippi Coast, according to WLOX-TV.

WLOX reported that someone found a threatening note along with a bullet inside a bathroom at the shipyard.

Authorities have not released the contents of the note.

Police said the investigation is ongoing, and no arrests have been announced.

Ingalls Shipbuilding told WLOX that operations at the facility are continuing as normal.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
SOUTHWEST MISSISSIPPI — Several southwest Mississippi counties are under burn bans as of Thursday morning, restricting all outdoor burning amid ongoing dry conditions.

According to county officials and the Mississippi Forestry Commission, burn bans are currently in effect for the following counties:

Scattered light rain showers could move across the region Thursday morning, with higher rain and thunderstorm chances expected late Thursday afternoon into the evening as a cold front pushes through the state. Isolated severe storms capable of producing hail up to quarter size are possible across parts of north and central Mississippi.

While rainfall in the forecast could improve conditions, officials caution that it may not be enough to immediately lift burn bans. Residents are urged to check the current status of their county’s burn ban before conducting any outdoor burning.

A burn ban is a restriction on outdoor burning during drought or heightened wildfire conditions. County boards of supervisors typically request burn bans, and the Mississippi Forestry Commission approves them. All burn bans expire at midnight on the stated end date unless extended.

Under a burn ban, no outdoor burning of any kind is allowed. The local sheriff’s department enforces burn bans, and anyone who knowingly and willfully violates a ban is guilty of a misdemeanor. Violators may face a fine of not less than $100 and not more than $500 under Section 49-19-351 of the Mississippi Code of 1972, as amended.

Items not allowed during a burn ban include campfires, bonfires, fire pits, fire rings, burn barrels, debris burning and field burning. Even small fires can pose a risk, as wind can carry embers up to one-half mile away and ignite spot fires.

Certain activities may be exempt if specifically listed in the county’s burn ban order. Possible exemptions can include certified burn managers, county fire services, commercial contractors using heavy construction equipment in compliance with Mississippi Department of Environmental Quality regulations, agricultural field burns and other designated exceptions.

Propane or gas grills and heaters and charcoal grills are generally allowed, provided they are used according to manufacturer instructions, kept away from combustible materials and never left unattended. Officials stress that charcoal briquettes should be allowed to cool completely and doused with water before being disposed of in a metal container. Ashes should be cold to the touch before disposal.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: SOUTHWEST MISSISSIPPI — Several southwest Mississippi counties are under burn bans as of Thursday morning, restricting all outdoor burning amid ongoing dry conditions.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Southwest Mississippi counties under burn ban as possible light rain moves in - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON — Three people were arrested Wednesday night after a driver fled from Capitol Police officers and crashed following a brief pursuit in Jackson.

Officers attempted to initiate a traffic stop at approximately 8:33 p.m. near John R. Lynch Street and Arbor Vista Boulevard on Feb. 25, according to authorities. The driver failed to stop, leading officers on a short pursuit that ended when the vehicle wrecked at the intersection of Grand Avenue and West Capitol Street.

Police said three individuals inside the vehicle were taken into custody at the scene without further incident.

The driver, 26-year-old Destiney Parks, was charged with felony fleeing.

A passenger, 31-year-old Keeunna Welch, was charged with disorderly conduct for failure to comply with lawful commands.

A second passenger, 35-year-old Jeremy Crump, was found to have an active warrant from the Ridgeland Police Department and was released to that agency’s custody.

No additional information was immediately released.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — Three people were arrested Wednesday night after a driver fled from Capitol Police officers and crashed following a brief pursuit in Jackson.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Three arrested after brief pursuit ends in crash in Jackson - Darkhorse Press

TUPELO- On February 18, Tupelo police officers responded to the North Mississippi Medical Center in reference to a previous sexual assault that allegedly happened at a Tupelo home.

The victim gave the officers the alleged suspect’s information.

On February 24, after further investigation, 34-year-old Juan Jose Cabral, from Tupelo, was arrested and charged with one count of Sexual Battery and one count of Rape.

On February 27, Tupelo Municipal Court Judge Jay Weird ordered Cabral to be held on a $40,000 bond. These charges will be presented to the Lee County Grand Jury.

JACKSON – Jackson Public Schools will observe Read Across Jackson Week March 2-6 after the City of Jackson issued a proclamation recognizing the literacy initiative.

Jackson Public Schools said Dr. Felicia Thomas, the district’s early childhood director of learning, and Ashanti Barnes, principal of Van Winkle Early Childhood Center, attended a City Hall meeting Feb. 24 to accept the proclamation on behalf of the school district.

District officials said the recognition reflects Jackson Public Schools’ ongoing focus on building strong literacy foundations for students and encouraging a community-wide love of reading.

Read Across Jackson Week is scheduled for March 2-6 in Jackson Public Schools.

PEARL — The City of Pearl will hold an Amnesty Day on Saturday, Feb. 28, offering residents with outstanding fines the opportunity to resolve contempt warrants without additional penalties, officials announced.

The event is scheduled from 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. at Pearl Court Records, 2561 Old Brandon Road. City officials said individuals who appear in person can have contempt warrants tied to unpaid fines removed, and any additional contempt fees forgiven.

The initiative is aimed at helping residents avoid escalating costs and legal consequences tied to unresolved violations.

Officials emphasized the event is not an enforcement tactic but an opportunity for residents to address outstanding issues voluntarily.

“This is not a trick or a ploy to bring people in and place them in custody,” officials said during a press conference announcing the event. “We want residents who believe they may have outstanding fines to come in and take care of them.”

Even those unable to pay the full amount are encouraged to attend and speak with court staff about available options. Officials said resolving warrants during Amnesty Day could prevent more serious consequences later, such as arrest during a traffic stop, vehicle towing and significantly higher costs.

Accepted forms of payment include cash, cashier’s checks, money orders and credit or debit cards. Personal checks and peer-to-peer payment services such as Cash App, Venmo and Apple Pay will not be accepted.

Residents seeking additional information can contact Pearl Court Records at 601-932-3581.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: PEARL — The City of Pearl will hold an Amnesty Day on Saturday, Feb. 28, offering residents with outstanding fines the opportunity to resolve contempt warrants without additional penalties, officials announced.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: City of Pearl to host Amnesty Day for residents with unpaid fines and contempt warrants - Darkhorse Press

CLINTON – The Clinton Police Department is asking for the public’s help identifying an individual in connection with multiple theft investigations within the city.

Police said several theft incidents have been reported over the past several months. Detectives obtained photographs of a person who may have information relevant to the cases, according to the department.

Investigators said the images are not clear enough to allow for positive identification, but they believe someone in the community may recognize the individual.

Anyone who recognizes the person or has information related to the theft investigations is asked to contact the Clinton Police Department at 601-924-5252. Tips may also be submitted anonymously through Crime Stoppers at 601-355-TIPS (8477).

Police said all information received will be reviewed as the investigations continue.

JACKSON – Crawfish will take center stage during CrawFest on Feb. 28, with an afternoon of food, live music, science programming and family activities.

Organizers said the event will run from noon to 5 p.m. and will feature games, interactive science stations and hands-on activities for all ages, along with local vendors.

Scheduled vendors include Mississippi Crawfish Co., KCrenshaw Art, MsT’s Sweets & Treats, Wholly Fire Hot Sauce and Watts Hollow Workshop, according to event information.

Live music is set to be provided by TB Ledford and the Accumulators. Food offerings are expected to include crawfish plates from Sal & Phil’s Seafood and Louisiana-style dishes from Dr. Flava’s Kitchen.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
RANKIN COUNTY: The owners of Finnegan Fire Safety Equipment have been sentenced after pleading guilty in a multi-county investigation into the sale and installation of faulty fire suppression systems in commercial kitchens across Mississippi, according to the Mississippi Office of the State Fire Marshal.

Daniel and Sherri Finnegan entered guilty pleas Tuesday in Rankin County. Daniel Finnegan was sentenced to 20 years, with seven years to serve. Sherri Finnegan was sentenced to five years, with four years suspended and one year to serve.

Authorities said the couple was arrested Aug. 21, 2024, after an investigation revealed they allegedly sold and installed used and defective fire suppression systems that were not properly tested before being placed into service. Officials said the systems did not work, putting customers at risk in the event of a fire.

Investigators said the couple sold systems to a number of businesses, including daycares and restaurants, and appear to have targeted minority-owned businesses and owners who had language barriers.

The plea agreements encompass charges filed in both Rankin and Madison counties. Across the two counties, 12 charges were filed against Daniel Finnegan and 12 against Sherri Finnegan stemming from six separate cases.

Originally, both were charged with 41 counts each of false pretense, a felony under Mississippi law, and 37 counts each of violations of the Mississippi Fire Prevention Code, which are misdemeanors.

Despite the guilty pleas in Rankin and Madison counties, officials said the couple still faces an additional 31 Fire Prevention Code violations and 37 false pretense charges that remain pending. The investigation involves 13 counties, with active charges remaining in 11 counties.

According to the fire marshal’s office, confirmed victims paid more than $343,000 directly to the couple. Officials said that figure does not reflect the total financial impact, as some documentation could not be obtained and many victims paid additional costs to correct deficient installations and bring their fire protection systems into compliance.

Restitution ordered for Daniel Finnegan totals $67,853.95, while restitution ordered for Sherri Finnegan totals $20,000.

The Mississippi State Fire Marshal’s Office said it will continue monitoring the remaining cases in other counties and provide updates as more information becomes available.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: RANKIN COUNTY: The owners of Finnegan Fire Safety Equipment have been sentenced after pleading guilty in a multi-county investigation into the sale and installation of faulty fire suppression systems in commercial kitchens across Mississippi, according to the Mississippi Office of the State Fire Marshal.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Finnegan Fire Safety targeted minority-owned, limited-English businesses with faulty systems - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON – A former federal attorney has been arrested and charged with arson after a building fire and a dumpster fire were reported early Friday on Hartsfield Street, according to the Jackson Fire Department.

Jackson firefighters responded around 12 a.m. Feb. 27 to 555 Hartsfield St. for a reported building fire and dumpster fire. Fire crews arrived and found two separate fires, the department said.

After the fires were extinguished, a fire investigator was called to the scene. Investigators spoke with Capitol Police, who had a suspect detained.

The Jackson Fire Department Arson Division arrested Yoder George McDowell III and charged him with two counts of arson.

Yoder George McDowell III is a former federal attorney.

Officials said McDowell is charged with first-degree arson related to the Yana Club and third-degree arson related to the dumpster. Authorities said the fires caused damage to the Yana Club and the dumpster. No injuries were reported.

The department said McDowell is scheduled to make an initial court appearance Friday at 10:30 a.m. at 327 E. Pascagoula St.

Jackson fire officials also noted that a fire at Pig in a Blanket referenced elsewhere was from a previous incident and did not occur Friday morning.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON – A former federal attorney has been arrested and charged with arson after a building fire and a dumpster fire were reported early Friday on Hartsfield Street, according to the Jackson Fire Department.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Former federal attorney charged with arson after building, dumpster fires in Fondren - Darkhorse Press

MADISON — A man was found dead inside a vehicle Friday morning in the parking lot of a Home Depot in Madison, according to police.

Officers with the Madison Police Department were called around 11:35 a.m. to the store at 205 Colony Way after receiving a report of a possibly deceased individual.

Responding officers located a male inside a truck that was backed into a parking space near the business and confirmed he was dead at the scene.

Investigators were called to begin a preliminary investigation into the manner of death. Authorities said the vehicle and the deceased have been transported to the State of Mississippi Crime Lab for further examination.

The man’s identity has not been released pending notification of next of kin.

The Madison County Coroner’s Office, the District Attorney’s Office and the Mississippi Bureau of Investigation are assisting with the investigation.

Officials said the case remains under investigation and additional details will be released when available.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Special to Darkhorse Press
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
CLINTON — A new partnership between Mississippi College and William Carey University allows students interested in pursuing a law degree access to an accelerated pathway.

A Memorandum of Understanding between WCU and the MC School of Law, signed Feb. 25, allows qualified William Carey students to earn both their bachelor’s degree and Juris Doctorate within six years of study, decreasing the total length of undergraduate studies plus law school by one full year.

“Through this partnership with William Carey University, we are expanding opportunities for students, strengthening legal education across our state, and helping ensure that future lawyers can pursue their calling and build their careers right here in Mississippi,” said MC President Blake Thompson.

MC Provost and Executive Vice President Mike Highfield said the agreement reflects MC’s commitment to building thoughtful partnerships that expand opportunity for students across Mississippi.

“By creating a streamlined pathway to the Juris Doctor, we are reducing both the time and cost required to earn a law degree while preserving the rigor and excellence of the MC School of Law,” Highfield said. “We are grateful for this collaboration and look forward to welcoming William Carey students to MC Law.”

Students who have completed three-fourths of the coursework required for a bachelor’s degree from WCU will be eligible for admission to law school. After the student successfully completes the first year of the JD program with a grade of C or higher in each course, WCU will assign academic credit based on the transfer equivalencies outlined in the participating degree program and award the appropriate bachelor’s degree.

After the student completes all law school requirements, MC will award the JD degree.

“We are always looking for ways to partner with other schools and organizations, which will benefit our students,” William Carey University President Ben Burnett said. “This partnership is particularly special because we are partnering with a sister Mississippi Baptist institution.”

Students participating in the accelerated degree program may earn an undergraduate degree in history, political science or administration of justice. They must take the Law School Admission Test and earn a competitive score no later than December of their third year of full-time enrollment at WCU.

Participating students must also apply to law school no later than February of the third year of undergraduate study using the standard process and criteria for law school admission.

Mississippi College School of Law Dean John Anderson said MC Law is a natural partner for William Carey University in their shared mission of providing a quality, faith-based education.

“This three plus three arrangement allows students to earn an undergraduate and law degree in six years rather than seven, saving a full year of tuition and expenses, while helping to keep Mississippi’s best and brightest future attorneys in our state,” Anderson said.

“This partnership between MC Law and WCU builds a pathway shaped by a Christian worldview that will give some of our brightest minds the opportunity to study law and practice in Mississippi,” said WCU Vice President for Academic Affairs and Provost Noal Cochran. “It’s an effort rooted in our shared Baptist faith and a commitment to providing quality education to our students – and opportunity to the clients they will serve.”

Transfer students are not eligible for the program: only incoming freshmen with a 26 ACT score or 1180 SAT score will qualify. Once admitted, students are expected to maintain at least a 3.25 GPA in their undergraduate work to remain competitive for early admission to the Mississippi College School of Law. Acceptance into the 3+3 program does not automatically guarantee admission to MC Law.

MC Law started educating future attorneys in 1930 as the Jackson School of Law in Jackson, Miss. The State Capitol, Mississippi Supreme Court, federal courts and many of Mississippi’s most respected law firms are just steps from the campus, giving students access to practical experience in the field before graduation.

For more information about MC Law’s 3+3 program, visit www.mc.edu/academics/departments/history/undergraduate-programs/3-3-law. ​​​​​​
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: CLINTON — A new partnership between Mississippi College and William Carey University allows students interested in pursuing a law degree access to an accelerated pathway.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Mississippi College partners with William Carey on accelerated law degree program - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON — The Mississippi Department of Wildlife, Fisheries, and Parks’ Mississippi Museum of Natural Science will host the inaugural CrawFest on Saturday, Feb. 28, inviting visitors to celebrate one of the state’s most recognizable native species through food, music and science.

The family-friendly event will take place from noon to 5 p.m. at the museum and will feature crawfish-themed cuisine, live music and hands-on educational activities exploring crawfish habitats and ecosystems across Mississippi.

Guests can purchase crawfish-inspired dishes from local food vendors, including Sal & Phil’s Seafood and Dr. Flava’s Louisiana Kitchen, while enjoying live entertainment from TB Ledford and the Accumulators, known for blending country blues, bluegrass and jug-band influences.

In addition to food and music, the festival will include interactive science stations, games and demonstrations led by area scientists highlighting crawfish diversity and their role in Mississippi’s ecosystems.

CrawFest is included with regular museum admission: $9 for adults, $7 for youth ages 3 to 18 and $8 for seniors 60 and older. Admission is free for Mississippi Museum of Natural Science Foundation members.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — The Mississippi Department of Wildlife, Fisheries, and Parks’ Mississippi Museum of Natural Science will host the inaugural CrawFest on Saturday, Feb. 28, inviting visitors to celebrate one of the state’s most recognizable native species through food, music and science.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Mississippi Museum of Natural Science to debut CrawFest celebrating state’s iconic crustacean - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard

RANKIN COUNTY – A narcotics investigation by the Rankin County Sheriff’s Department has resulted in the arrest of a man accused of selling methamphetamine near a church.

Investigators with the sheriff’s department narcotics unit arrested Gavin Lamon Walker on a charge of sale of a controlled substance within 1,500 feet of a church, according to the department.

Officials said Walker’s arrest stems from the sale of methamphetamine in multiple quantities. The investigation remains ongoing and additional charges or arrests are possible.

After his arrest, Walker was taken to the Rankin County Adult Detention Center. The sheriff’s department said he made an initial appearance this week before Rankin County Court Judge David Morrow Jr., who set bond at $50,000.

“It always has been and always will be my priority to keep Rankin County safe from those that would poison it with drugs,” Sheriff Bryan Bailey said in a statement.

The sheriff’s department also thanked the Pearl Police Department for assisting in the investigation and arrest.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: RANKIN COUNTY – A narcotics investigation by the Rankin County Sheriff’s Department has resulted in the arrest of a man accused of selling methamphetamine near a church.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Rankin County narcotics unit arrests man accused of selling meth near church - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
COVINGTON COUNTY — The Mississippi Bureau of Investigation has issued a Silver Alert for a missing Covington County man believed to be at risk.

According to MBI, 31-year-old Cornelius Elijah Allen of Collins was last seen around 2:39 a.m. Wednesday, Feb. 25, in the 200 block of Monroe Road in Covington County. He was driving in an unknown direction.

Allen is described as 5 feet 9 inches tall, weighing approximately 170 pounds, with black hair and brown eyes. He was last seen wearing blue jeans, a blue shirt and a black hoodie.

Investigators believe Allen may be traveling in a 2017 black Volkswagen Tiguan bearing Mississippi tag 910 CVB.

Family members told authorities that Allen suffers from a medical condition that may impair his judgment.

Anyone who sees Allen or has information about his whereabouts is asked to contact the Covington County Sheriff’s Office at 601-765-8281 or call 911.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: COVINGTON COUNTY — The Mississippi Bureau of Investigation has issued a Silver Alert for a missing Covington County man believed to be at risk.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Silver Alert issued for missing Covington County man - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON — State Farm’s Board of Directors has approved a cash-back dividend for eligible private passenger auto policies. Mississippi policyholders who had an active policy as of December 31, 2025, will receive a share of more than $70.5 million, averaging about $124 per vehicle.

“This announcement is welcome news for Mississippi drivers. Returning millions of dollars directly to policyholders, on top of meaningful rate reductions, translates into real relief for consumers. Actions like this help stabilize the auto insurance market while ensuring companies remain financially sound and able to meet their long-term obligations to policyholders,” said Mississippi Insurance Commissioner Mike Chaney.

Nationally, State Farm Mutual has announced $5 billion in cash back to auto consumers, the largest dividend in company history. Distributions begin this summer.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — State Farm’s Board of Directors has approved a cash-back dividend for eligible private passenger auto policies. Mississippi policyholders who had an active policy as of December 31, 2025, will receive a share of more than $70.5 million, averaging about $124 per vehicle.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: State Farm Gives $70.5 Million Cash Back to Mississippi Drivers - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON – The G.V. (Sonny) Montgomery VA Medical Center recognized “Thank a Resident Day” and encouraged patients and staff to show appreciation for resident physicians who care for veterans while completing medical training.

In a social media post, the medical center said resident physicians are doctors in training who work long shifts and manage complex cases while providing care with compassion and focus.

The medical center also highlighted the VA’s role in training the nation’s health care workforce. According to the post, the Department of Veterans Affairs is the largest provider of health professions training in the United States, training more than 120,000 health professionals each year, including about 40,000 resident physicians.

The medical center said nearly 70% of U.S. physicians complete part of their training at a VA facility during their careers, meaning many community health care providers have trained in the VA system.

Officials said supporting VA training programs helps not only today’s veterans but also the future of health care nationwide.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON – The G.V. (Sonny) Montgomery VA Medical Center recognized “Thank a Resident Day” and encouraged patients and staff to show appreciation for resident physicians who care for veterans while completing medical training.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: VA medical center marks “Thank a Resident Day,” highlights role of physicians in training - Darkhorse Press

WIGGINS — The Wiggins Police Department is asking for the public’s help in identifying a suspect connected to a recent incident in the city.

Authorities released images of the individual this week in hopes that someone in the community may recognize them and provide information.

Anyone with information about the suspect is urged to contact the Wiggins Police Department at 601-928-5444.

Tips can also be submitted anonymously through Crime Stoppers using an online tip form, according to police.

Officials thanked residents for their continued support in assisting efforts to keep the community safe.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: WIGGINS — The Wiggins Police Department is asking for the public’s help in identifying a suspect connected to a recent incident in the city.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Wiggins police seek public’s help identifying suspect in recent incident - Darkhorse Press

JEFFERSON COUNTY – Two teenagers were killed in a crash in Jefferson County Friday morning, February 28.

According to MHP, the crash happened at 8:52 a.m. on Highway 552 West in Jefferson County.

A 2018 Nissan Altima driven by 18-year-old Kamarion J. Hyder of Port Gibson, MS, and passenger, 19-year-old Dyamond Dee of Natchez, MS, traveled east on Highway 552 West when it left the roadway, and collided with a tree.

Both Kamarion J. Hyder and Dyamond Dee received fatal injuries from the crash and were pronounced dead at the scene.

This crash remains under investigation by the Mississippi Highway Patrol.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JEFFERSON COUNTY – Two teenagers were killed in a crash in Jefferson County Friday morning, February 28.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: 18 and 19-year-old killed in Jefferson County crash - Darkhorse Press

GREENVILLE – The Greenville Police Department is asking for the public’s help in locating a 16-year-old runaway juvenile.

According to police, Madison Brady, 16, was last seen leaving her home Saturday evening around 9 p.m. in Greenville. Authorities said it is unknown what she was wearing at the time.

Brady is described as a Black female with a medium complexion, standing 5 feet 5 inches tall and weighing approximately 165 pounds. She has black, medium-length hair and brown eyes.

Police said anyone who knows Brady’s whereabouts is urged to contact the Greenville Police Department at 662-378-1515, extension 1, or Crime Stoppers at 662-378-8477.

The case remains under investigation.

JACKSON – Jackson police are investigating a double homicide after two men were found dead late Sunday night in the 5300 block of Dogwood Trail near Oakridge Road.

According to police, officers with Precinct 1 were dispatched around 10 p.m. March 2 in response to a reported shooting. Upon arrival, officers located two deceased individuals: a black male lying in a ditch and a white male lying in the street nearby.

Authorities identified the victims as 31-year-old Antonio Arnold and 63-year-old Glen McWilliams. Police said next of kin for both men have been notified.

The Jackson Police Department’s Robbery/Homicide Unit and Mobile Crime Unit responded to the scene, along with the Hinds County Coroner’s Office.

The case remains under active investigation. Anyone with information is asked to contact the Jackson Police Department at 601-960-1234 or Crime Stoppers at 601-355-TIPS (8477).
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON – Jackson police are investigating a double homicide after two men were found dead late Sunday night in the 5300 block of Dogwood Trail near Oakridge Road.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Jackson Police Investigating Double Homicide on Dogwood Trail - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
BENTON COUNTY — It was a hot Mississippi day in Michigan City when L.G. Shaw, a 76-year-old farmer and retired trucking company owner, disappeared into thin air.

Shaw has been missing since July 15, 2009, after disappearing from his property in Benton County. He was last seen that morning at his granddaughter’s home, wearing a plaid shirt and khaki pants. Family members say he left after his usual visit and never returned.

According to his family, Shaw kept a consistent daily routine. Each morning he fed his livestock and made rounds to visit family members who lived within a 3-mile radius. He repeated the same routine in the evenings, often stopping back by his granddaughter’s home around dinnertime.

On the morning he vanished, Shaw told his granddaughter he had been unable to locate one of his colts and believed the animal may have been hung up somewhere. He left to check on it but did not come back that evening.

Concerned when he failed to return and did not answer phone calls, his granddaughter went to his home a short distance away. She found his truck parked at the entrance to his pasture, but Shaw was nowhere around the vehicle.

Inside the home, she found his cigarettes and cell phone on his nightstand — items he always carried in the front pocket of his shirt. Family members say Shaw also routinely carried two wallets, one of which contained a large sum of money. Both wallets were missing.

Shaw’s home is located in Benton County but his pasture extends across the state line into Hardeman County, Tennessee. His granddaughter initially attempted to report him missing in Hardeman County, but authorities determined Benton County had jurisdiction because that is where his home is located.

Family members, volunteers and a Tennessee-based search organization canvassed a 7-mile radius around Shaw’s home for seven days. No trace of him was found.

Shaw is described as a Black male who was 76 years old at the time of his disappearance. He was last seen wearing a plaid shirt and khaki pants.

Anyone with information about L.G. Shaw’s disappearance is urged to contact his granddaughter, D’Aunna, at 731-609-9570.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: BENTON COUNTY — It was a hot Mississippi day in Michigan City when L.G. Shaw, a 76-year-old farmer and retired trucking company owner, disappeared into thin air.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: MISSING IN MISSISSIPPI: L.G. Shaw, Benton County - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard

NATCHEZ — Firefighters responded late Saturday night to a fully engulfed mobile home fire on Lower Woodville Road in Adams County.

Adams County Central Dispatch received a 911 call at approximately 11:14 p.m. March 1 reporting a structure fire at 806 Lower Woodville Road. The caller said that a mobile home was on fire.

The Natchez Fire Department responded to the scene, along with additional units. Deputies with the Adams County Sheriff’s Office also responded to assist with scene security and traffic control.

When first responders arrived, they found the mobile home fully engulfed in flames. Firefighters remained on scene working to contain and extinguish the fire and to prevent it from spreading to nearby areas.

Officials said there were no confirmed reports of injuries as of early Sunday.

The cause of the fire remains under investigation.

Residents were asked to avoid the area of Lower Woodville Road while emergency crews worked.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: NATCHEZ — Firefighters responded late Saturday night to a fully engulfed mobile home fire on Lower Woodville Road in Adams County.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Mobile home destroyed by fire in Adams County - Darkhorse Press

NEWTON – A traffic stop for a noise ordinance violation late Friday night led to multiple charges against a Newton man, according to the Newton Police Department.

Police said officers conducted a traffic stop during the late hours of Feb. 28 in the area of Martin Luther King Jr. Drive for a violation of the City of Newton’s noise ordinance.

Following the stop, the driver was identified as Richard Russell. Authorities said Russell has been charged with violating the city’s noise ordinance, driving with a suspended license, no insurance and being a convicted felon in possession of a firearm.

“The Newton Police Department will continue to enforce the city’s ordinances and state laws no matter how big or small they are,” Chief McKenzie Patrick said in a statement.

No additional details were released.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: NEWTON – A traffic stop for a noise ordinance violation late Friday night led to multiple charges against a Newton man, according to the Newton Police Department.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Newton man charged with weapons, traffic offenses after late-night stop - Darkhorse Press

GULF PARK ESTATES — One man has been arrested and another is wanted following a shooting Thursday night in Gulf Park Estates, according to the Jackson County Sheriff’s Department.

Sheriff John Ledbetter said deputies responded at approximately 9:30 p.m. Feb. 26 to a reported shooting near North 19th Street and Pickering Forrest Drive.

When deputies arrived, they found 47-year-old Nathan Lee suffering from two gunshot wounds following an altercation with another party. Lee was transported to a local hospital for treatment and is listed in stable condition.

During the investigation, authorities identified two suspects: 46-year-old Christopher Paul Landry and 32-year-old Glen Guy Barton.

Investigators arrested Barton on one count of aggravated assault with a deadly weapon. Landry is wanted on an active warrant for aggravated assault with a deadly weapon, and his whereabouts are unknown.

The case remains under active investigation, and additional charges or arrests may be forthcoming, officials said.

Anyone with information about the incident is urged to contact the Jackson County Sheriff’s Department at 228-769-3063 or Crime Stoppers at 877-787-5898.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: GULF PARK ESTATES — One man has been arrested and another is wanted following a shooting Thursday night in Gulf Park Estates, according to the Jackson County Sheriff’s Department.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: One arrested, one wanted after shooting in Gulf Park Estates - Darkhorse Press

OXFORD: Oxford has been nominated for the USA TODAY 10Best Readers’ Choice Awards in the Best Small College Town category, Visit Oxford MS announced Monday.

Voting is open now and will remain open until noon Eastern time on Monday, March 30. Readers may vote once per category, per day.

“We’re thrilled to see Oxford nominated again for the Best Small College Town category,” said Kinney Ferris, executive director of Visit Oxford MS. “This continued recognition of our beloved college town is a celebration of the unique relationship between the town and the University in an environment that blends academics, athletics, arts and culture.”

Nominees in all categories are selected by a panel of experts, including editors from USA TODAY and 10Best.com.

According to Visit Oxford MS, 10Best.com is known for its original and experiential travel content developed by local travel experts and highlights top attractions, activities and restaurants in destinations across the United States and around the world.

Readers can cast their votes for Oxford online through the USA TODAY 10Best website.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: OXFORD: Oxford has been nominated for the USA TODAY 10Best Readers’ Choice Awards in the Best Small College Town category, Visit Oxford MS announced Monday.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Oxford nominated for USA TODAY 10Best Best Small College Town award - Darkhorse Press

RANKIN COUNTY — A registered sex offender who authorities say had absconded was arrested Friday after investigators determined he was living at an unreported address in Rankin County.

Investigators with the Rankin County Sheriff’s Department said they received information Feb. 27 that Phillip Marvin Winter, a registered sex offender, had absconded and was residing at an unknown location within the county.

After further investigation, deputies confirmed Winter was living at a residence in the Plantation Shore subdivision along Highway 49 south of Florence.

Authorities said Winter was originally ordered to register as a sex offender after pleading guilty to statutory rape in 2008. He was later convicted in 2021 of failure to register as a sex offender.

Following his arrest, Winter was transported to the Rankin County Adult Detention Center, where he is being held on a $5,000 bond pending his initial appearance before David Morrow Jr..

No additional information was immediately released.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: RANKIN COUNTY — A registered sex offender who authorities say had absconded was arrested Friday after investigators determined he was living at an unreported address in Rankin County.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Registered sex offender arrested in Rankin County after allegedly absconding - Darkhorse Press

According to MBI, 65-year-old Larry Layton was last seen Sunday, March 1, at about 1 a.m. in the 3700 block of Capitol Street in Hinds County. Authorities said he was walking in an unknown direction and it is not known what he was wearing when he was last seen.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
CAMDEN — Twelve years after 2-year-old Myra Lewis vanished from her family’s yard in Madison County, the case remains open, the reward remains unclaimed and investigators say the answers are still out there.

This week, the Federal Bureau of Investigation renewed its appeal, offering a reward of up to $20,000 for information regarding her whereabouts. A post from the FBI Sunday served as a reminder of the anniversary so many in the Metro area mark each year, even as the missing posters still hang in the Madison County Sheriff’s Department.

“The #FBI is offering a reward of up to $20,000 for information regarding the whereabouts of Myra Lewis, #missing from Camden, Mississippi, since March 1, 2014: https://www.fbi.gov/wanted/kidnap/myra-lewis ,” it states.

Myra was last seen between 10:30 and 11 a.m. on March 1, 2014, playing outside her home in Camden. She was wearing white or khaki pants, a turquoise sweater with a bear on the front and pink tennis shoes. At the time, she was 3 feet, 1 inch tall and weighed 27 pounds, with black hair and brown eyes.

The child’s disappearance is Mississippi’s longest-standing Amber Alert case. Despite massive searches, national attention and years of investigation, no one has been charged and no trace of the toddler has ever been found.

In 2025, Randy Tucker spoke publicly in unprecedented detail on the “Investigation: Homicide” podcast about the day Myra disappeared and the suspicions that have lingered since.

From the beginning, Tucker said, certain elements of the scene troubled investigators, particularly the response of tracking dogs brought to the property.

A tracking dog refused to leave the immediate area of the yard where Myra had been playing, Tucker said. A cadaver dog later alerted to the back of a side-by-side ATV on the property. That was a detail the sheriff described as “very strange,” given there was no clear explanation for Myra’s scent to be there.

Searchers combed the area for days, dragging ponds, searching woods and even examining recent burial sites. Volunteers and law enforcement from local, state and federal agencies participated. Still, Myra was “gone like a breath in the wind,” Tucker said.

SEE MYRA LEWIS in MISSING IN MISSISSIPPI

Over the years, the sheriff has grown increasingly convinced that the truth lies within a very small circle.

“I firmly believe to this very date that the people that were on that property that day know exactly what happened, know exactly where Myra is, and the circumstances surrounding her disappearance,” Tucker said on the podcast.

He pointed to inconsistencies in statements about Myra’s whereabouts in the days before she was reported missing, including conflicting accounts about whether she attended school the day before. He also noted what he described as unusual behavior from family members in the immediate aftermath and in the years since.

Attempts by media, including Darkhorse Press, to contact family members over the years have gone unanswered. Tucker has said he believes someone may eventually come forward.

“Until somebody clears their conscience, we’re no closer than we were,” he said.

The case remains active. Authorities continue to receive occasional tips from across the country, though none have led to a breakthrough.

The FBI urges anyone with information about Myra Lewis to contact their local FBI office, the nearest American Embassy or Consulate, or submit an anonymous tip online. Officials say the $20,000 reward remains available for information leading to her whereabouts.

Twelve years later, the smiling toddler in pink remains frozen in time on missing posters, and investigators say they are still waiting for the piece of the puzzle that completes the picture of what happened to little Myra.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: CAMDEN — Twelve years after 2-year-old Myra Lewis vanished from her family’s yard in Madison County, the case remains open, the reward remains unclaimed and investigators say the answers are still out there.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Twelve years later: Myra Lewis’ disappearance still haunts Mississippi - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON — Clinics at the University of Mississippi Medical Center reopened today and resumed normal operations and appointment schedules following a cyberattack that disrupted network systems last month.

The medical center announced that all clinics are back to regular hours as of Monday and will begin rescheduling patient appointments that were canceled after the Feb. 19 attack. Officials said clinics will operate extended hours and additional days in the coming weeks to accommodate as many impacted patients as possible.

“It will take some time to fully recover and to investigate this criminal attack on our network systems. This was not just an attack on UMMC; it was also an attack on our patients, students, faculty and staff,” said Dr. LouAnn Woodward, vice chancellor for health affairs. “Thank you to our patients and their families for their patience as we worked diligently to restore access to the services that they need.”

Patients regained access to their MyChart accounts over the weekend as the electronic health record system came back online Saturday. With phone lines and internet service restored, UMMC has also resumed regular patient call center operations.

The UMMC Triage Line will remain open for a few days this week before calls are transitioned back to the staffed call center. Patients with time-sensitive medical needs can contact the triage line at 601-815-0000.

Hospital officials said recovery efforts are ongoing as they continue investigating the cyberattack and working to fully restore all systems.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — Clinics at the University of Mississippi Medical Center reopened today and resumed normal operations and appointment schedules following a cyberattack that disrupted network systems last month.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: UMMC clinics reopen today after cyberattack; normal operations resume - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.author: Special to Darkhorse Press
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.content_html:
By Kelli Sharpe Jackson State University

JACKSON — Today, in celebration of Black History Month and in anticipation of the worldwide theatrical release of “MICHAEL,” Jackson State University’s Sonic Boom of the South steps into the national spotlight once again with a powerful performance of Michael Jackson’s classic hit “Don’t Stop Til You Get Enough,” blending JSU’s rich heritage with the pop icon’s unmistakable groove.

“Michael Jackson’s global impact on music and culture is undeniable,” said Jackson State University Interim President Denise Jones Gregory, Ph.D. “Featuring the Sonic Boom of the South in this national campaign reflects the level of excellence, discipline and tradition that define Jackson State University and HBCUs nationwide. We are proud to see our students represent that legacy on a national stage.”

The Sonic Boom, celebrated as the summa cum laude of bands, is no stranger to the limelight. However, being a part of this initiative has a special meaning for senior Desi Jenkins, a music education major and lead drum major of the “Jackson 5”, fondly known as the J5.

“Throughout my entire career as a student, I’ve performed Michael’s selections and dance routines, with the most recent being our Homecoming 2024 halftime show,” Jenkins said. “I choreographed and facilitated my entire ‘Thriller’ segment for the show. Michael is and will always be my favorite artist, humanitarian and inspiration. Being part of the campaign of this film is extremely full-circle and everything greater than special to me. I am absolutely humbled and honored!”

“It is always a tremendous honor to represent our institution and band program on a national stage,” Roderick Little, Ph.D., band director of the Sonic Boom added. “This opportunity and iconic moment carries even greater significance as we celebrate the legacy of Michael Jackson, arguably one of the greatest entertainers of all time. Most importantly, this experience represents a milestone that our students will cherish for a lifetime. It is an achievement they will proudly look back on and say they were part of something truly special.”

“MICHAEL CELEBRATES: Legacy, Artistry, Culture,” pays tribute to Jackson’s legacy through powerful performances rooted in culture, tradition and pride. The initiative reflects the shared, enduring relationship between the HBCU community and Michael’s life and music.

“For Michael’s fans everywhere, his legacy of performance and artistry is enduring. That legacy lives powerfully within HBCU communities, where music, movement and excellence have long been expressions of culture, pride and identity,” said Briana McElroy, head of digital marketing for the Lionsgate Motion Picture Group. “This initiative is deeply personal to me. I grew up listening to these bands that represent Black excellence, community and creativity. Partnering with these HBCUs is about honoring that legacy while creating space for the next generation to lead, perform and inspire.”

You can see The Sonic Boom’s performance now on all JSU social media platforms and across the “MICHAEL” movie socials. “MICHAEL” releases in theaters everywhere April 24, 2026.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.summary: By Kelli Sharpe Jackson State University
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.title: JSU’s Sonic Boom proudly joins “MICHAEL CELEBRATES: Legacy, Artistry, Culture” - Darkhorse Press
